content:
# Genesis Chapter 40 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23r–23v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_040_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_040_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0042 (page 38), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0043 (page 39)

---

## The Man Who Interprets

**1** Some time after these things, the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and the king's baker gave offense to their master — the king of Egypt. **2** Pharaoh was furious with his two officials: the chief cupbearer and the chief baker. **3** He placed them under arrest in the house of the captain of the guard — in the same prison where Joseph was confined. **4** The captain of the guard assigned Joseph to wait on them, and he attended to them. They had been in custody for some time.

---

## The Troubled Face

**5** Then on the same night, both of them dreamed — the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, each man his dream, each dream with its own meaning.

**6** When Joseph came to them in the morning, he looked at them and saw that they were troubled. **7** He asked Pharaoh's officials who were in custody with him in his master's house, "Why do you look so downcast today?"

**8** They said to him, "We had dreams, and there is no one to interpret them."

Joseph said to them, "Don't interpretations belong to God? Tell me."

---

## The Cupbearer's Dream

**9** So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream. He said, "In my dream — there was a vine in front of me. **10** On the vine were three branches. It was as if it was budding — and in no time its blossoms had opened into clusters of ripe grapes. **11** Pharaoh's cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes, pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand."

---

## Joseph Interprets: Restoration

**12** Joseph said to him, "This is what it means. The three branches are three days. **13** In just three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your position. You will place Pharaoh's cup in his hand, just as you used to do when you were his cupbearer.

**14** "But if it goes well for you — please, do something for me. Remember me. Show me covenant-faithfulness and mention me to Pharaoh. Get me out of this place. **15** Because I was, in fact, stolen from the land of the Hebrews, and here too I have done nothing that they should throw me into this pit."

---

## The Baker's Dream

**16** When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he said to Joseph, "I also had a dream. There were three baskets of white bread on my head. **17** In the top basket were all kinds of baked goods for Pharaoh — and the birds were eating them out of the basket on my head."

---

## Joseph Interprets: Execution

**18** Joseph answered, "This is what it means. The three baskets are three days. **19** In just three days Pharaoh will lift your head — from off you. He will hang you on a tree, and the birds will eat your flesh."

---

## The Birthday — and the Forgetting

**20** Now the third day was Pharaoh's birthday, and he gave a feast for all his servants. He lifted up the head of the chief cupbearer and the head of the chief baker in the presence of all his servants. **21** He restored the chief cupbearer to his position, so that he placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand. **22** But the chief baker he hanged — just as Joseph had said in his interpretation of them.

**23** Yet the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph. He forgot him.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### "Do not interpretations belong to God?" — the theological hinge

**הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים** — "Do not interpretations belong to God?"

This is the theological center of the chapter, and it arrives in a rhetorical question — the most underplayed possible form for the most consequential statement Joseph has made yet. He does not announce himself as a prophet. He does not claim special powers. He does not charge a fee or demand to be brought to the king. He says: *interpretations belong to God — now tell me.*

The word **פִּתְרֹנִים** (*pitronim*) — interpretations, the thing dreams need in order to become legible — appears here for the first time in the narrative. Its root **פָּתַר** (*patar*) is not common in biblical Hebrew; it appears almost exclusively in the Joseph cycle (Genesis 40–41), where it is the key technical term for unlocking the meaning of a dream. The narrator uses it as a kind of password for this sequence of the story: where you find *patar*, you are in Joseph's domain.

The rhetorical question **הֲלוֹא** (*halo'*) — "Is it not the case that...?" — expects the answer yes. Joseph is not musing; he is asserting. The form of the assertion is humble (he does not say *I* interpret, *I* know, *I* have special access), but the claim is enormous. He is saying: God is the source of all dream-meaning, and I have access to that source. Come to me.

This is Joseph's first explicit speech in which he presents himself as a conduit for divine communication. In chapter 37, the dreams came to him. In chapter 39, he is defined by his integrity and his work-ethic. Here, in chapter 40, he steps forward as an interpreter — someone who stands between the encrypted language of the night and the plain speech of the day, and translates. It is a role he will reprise with Pharaoh in chapter 41, and the language will echo: **בִּלְעָדָי לֹא אֲנִי** — "not I, but God" (41:16). The pattern is established here, in a prison, with two anxious officials of a foreign king, over morning rounds.

The theology implicit in the claim is striking. The world is full of dream-professionals in the ancient Near East — haruspices, oneiromancers, interpreters-for-hire in every court. Joseph's claim is not that he is a better technician. It is that the entire enterprise of interpretation belongs to a different domain than human expertise. You cannot purchase this. You cannot train for it. You can only receive it, from God, when God chooses to give it.

---

### "I was indeed stolen" — Joseph's self-narration

**כִּי-גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים** — "Because I was, in fact, stolen from the land of the Hebrews."

This is the first and only time in the entire Joseph narrative that Joseph tells his own story from his own perspective. Everything we know about what happened to him — the pit, the sale, the brothers, the Ishmaelites — we know from the narrator's account. Joseph himself has been strikingly silent on the subject. He has not complained to Potiphar, has not appealed his imprisonment, has not narrated his past to the prison warden. He has simply worked.

And now, in a prison, to a stranger who might have some access to Pharaoh, Joseph speaks. The construction is an intensifying infinitive absolute: **גֻּנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי** — *stolen, I was stolen* — the same kind of emphasis we find in 37:33 when Jacob says **טָרֹף טֹרַף** (*torn, he was torn!*). The doubled verbal form functions as an assertion against denial: *I am not here because of anything I did. I was stolen. This is not ambiguous.*

The word Joseph chooses is **גָּנַב** — to steal, to take by stealth. This is not the word for violent capture; it is the word for taking something that belongs to someone else by deception or in secret. Joseph is using the language of theft to describe his own trafficking — and he is right. He was not taken in war, not sold by a father who could not feed him, not voluntarily indentured. He was lured out to his brothers under the pretense of a family visit and sold for silver while his father sat in Hebron thinking him dead.

The verb **גָּנַב** also carries an irony the original audience would have heard: this is the same verb used in Genesis 31:20 when Jacob "stole the heart" of Laban by leaving secretly, and in 31:26-27 when Laban accuses Jacob of stealing away. The word runs in Jacob's family — they are people who deceive, who steal, who take things by misdirection. Joseph has been the victim of his own family's instinct for cunning, turned against him.

The phrase **מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים** — "from the land of the Hebrews" — is also unusual. This is the only occurrence of "land of the Hebrews" in the entire Bible. Normally we would say "land of Canaan" or "land of my fathers." Joseph says *the land of the Hebrews* — positioning himself as a member of an ethnic community whose homeland he has been violently removed from. He is not simply a prisoner who claims innocence; he is a displaced person asserting a national identity and a right of belonging somewhere.

---

### The bor — the pit that echoes

**לַבּוֹר** — "into the pit."

The Hebrew word **בּוֹר** (*bor*) means pit, cistern, dungeon. It appears in verse 15 when Joseph says "they threw me into this pit" (*bor*), and the reader who has been following carefully hears an immediate echo.

In Genesis 37:20, when the brothers plot to kill Joseph, they say "let us throw him into one of the pits" (*habborot*). In 37:22, Reuben says "throw him into this pit in the wilderness" (*habor hazeh*). In 37:24, "they threw him into the pit (*habor*) — and the pit was empty, with no water in it."

Joseph uses the same word now — **בּוֹר** — for the Egyptian prison. The word choice is not accidental. For Joseph, the Egyptian prison is not a different category of suffering from the pit in which his brothers left him: it is the *same* pit. The descent that began in a dry cistern in the wilderness of Dothan has continued into a royal dungeon in Egypt. The bor is his condition, not a specific location. He has been in the bor — in various forms — since chapter 37.

The word *bor* also carries cosmic resonance. In the Psalms, the *bor* is frequently a metaphor for Sheol, the realm of death: Psalm 88:4-6 places the speaker "in the pit" alongside the dead, cut off from the living. Psalm 28:1 fears "going down to the pit." The word sits at the intersection of physical imprisonment and symbolic death — and Joseph's use of it to describe his current situation while also reaching back to his past suggests that he understands his entire trajectory as a sustained death. He has been in the bor since his brothers threw him in.

The theology that the Joseph narrative is building — quietly, without announcement — is that the bor is not the end. That is what chapter 40 is beginning to establish, even as it ends with Joseph still confined: the dream is the vehicle of ascent from the bor, and Joseph is the man who can decode the dreams.

---

### "Lift up your head" — the terrible double meaning

**יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ** — "Pharaoh will lift up your head."

Joseph uses this phrase twice — once for the cupbearer (v.13) and once for the baker (v.19). The repetition is exact, the idiom identical, but the second instance adds four devastating words: **מֵעָלֶיךָ** — "from off you."

"Pharaoh will lift up your head — and restore you to your position" (v.13). Good news, idiomatic expression: to lift someone's head is to restore their dignity, to reinstate them, to grant them honor after a period of shame. It is a phrase of elevation.

"Pharaoh will lift up your head — from off you" (v.19). The same idiom, but now completed in a way that makes it literal and lethal. Pharaoh will lift the baker's head — by separating it from his body. The phrase that was metaphorical becomes grimly physical. The interpretation is announced in the same grammatical form as the good news, and the baker only realizes the reversal when the four added words land.

This is one of the most chilling interpretive moments in all of Hebrew narrative. The narrator and Joseph both use the same words for restoration and execution, and the difference is not in the grammar but in a four-word addition that turns the idiom inside out. The baker heard the cupbearer's interpretation and thought: *the language is the same; I will receive the same good news.* He did not. The language was the same because Pharaoh acts in both cases — he lifts heads. But the nature of the lifting is entirely different.

Joseph delivers this oracle without recorded hesitation. He does not soften it, does not perform reluctance, does not hedge. He was asked for an interpretation; he gives one. The theological frame established in verse 8 — interpretations belong to God — means that Joseph cannot be held responsible for the content of the interpretation. He is a conduit, not an author. But the weight of delivering a death sentence to a man who was hoping for restoration cannot have been light to carry. The text is silent on Joseph's interior response, as it is silent on so many things. We watch him speak the truth and then the narrative moves on.

---

### Pharaoh's birthday — death and restoration in the same feast

**וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת-פַּרְעֹה** — "Now on the third day it was Pharaoh's birthday."

The birthday feast appears without preparation or warning. The narrative has given us the dreams, the interpretations, and then — three days later — the event that will prove or disprove the interpretations. The structure mirrors the interpretive structure of the chapter: a dream, an interpretation, a fulfillment. The fulfillment arrives as a feast.

Pharaoh's birthday (*yom huledeth* — "day of his birth") is the only birthday celebration mentioned in the Hebrew Bible until Herod's banquet in the New Testament (Matthew 14:6; Mark 6:21), where it is also the occasion for an execution. The coincidence is unlikely to be accidental: the pattern of a royal birthday celebration resulting in a beheading appears to have been a known cultural type in the ancient Near East, and the New Testament narrator may well have been writing with this episode in mind.

The phrase **וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-רֹאשׁ** — "he lifted up the head" — appears twice in verse 20, once for each official. The narrator uses the same idiom Joseph used in his interpretations, now reporting the fulfillment. The word has arrived. What was declared in the prison has happened in the palace. The interpretation was accurate.

The narrator says Pharaoh "lifted up the head" of the cupbearer among his servants — and restored him. And "lifted up the head" of the baker — and hanged him. Both liftings. One idiom, two outcomes. The narrator echoes Joseph's double use of the phrase deliberately, completing the circuit between prophecy and event.

---

### "The chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph. He forgot him." — the cruelty of the final verse

**וְלֹא-זָכַר שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת-יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ** — "But the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph — he forgot him."

The chapter ends with two verbs and a silence.

**לֹא זָכַר** — "did not remember" — the negative of *zakhar*, to remember, to call to mind, to act on the basis of remembrance. In Hebrew, *zakhar* is not merely a cognitive event; it implies action. When God "remembers" Noah (8:1), he sends the wind. When God "remembers" Rachel (30:22), she conceives. To remember, in biblical Hebrew, is to respond. The cupbearer did not respond. He did not act on what he knew about Joseph.

**וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ** — "and he forgot him." This second verb is not a mere repetition of the first; it adds its own weight. The forgetting — *shakhach* — is active and complete. Not "he failed to remember" but "he forgot." The memory was not merely unacted upon; it was lost. Joseph — who had interpreted his dream, who had asked to be mentioned to Pharaoh, who had explained his innocence and his theft — simply slipped out of the cupbearer's mind as the cupbearer walked back into his old life.

This is the most devastating sentence in the chapter, and the narrator delivers it in the flattest possible register. No commentary. No authorial judgment. No prediction of Joseph's future vindication. Just: he forgot him.

The reader who has been tracking the theological argument of these chapters knows that two years will pass (41:1) before Pharaoh's dreams revive the cupbearer's memory. Two years of additional imprisonment for Joseph — years not recorded, not narrated, years that pass in the gap between 40:23 and 41:1 as a white space in the text. He will spend that time in a place that no one important seems to know he is, on the basis of a request that no one remembered to make, because of a promise that was forgotten before sundown.

And yet — the theology the narrator has been building does not allow the forgetting to be the final word. The text that says "the LORD was with Joseph" in chapter 39 does not revise that claim in chapter 40 when the cupbearer forgets. The cupbearer's memory and God's are not the same thing. What the cupbearer fails to hold, God has not released. The forgetting is real; the abandonment is not.

The structure of the Joseph narrative, viewed from the end, makes clear that the cupbearer's forgetting is not a tragic derailment of Providence but a precise component of it. If the cupbearer had remembered immediately, Joseph would have been mentioned to Pharaoh as a curiosity — *there's a clever Hebrew prisoner who interpreted my dream.* Pharaoh would have no urgent reason to act. But two years later, when Pharaoh himself has dreamed and all Egypt's wise men have failed him, the cupbearer's memory surfaces in a context where it has maximum force: *I know a man.* The moment of remembering is the right moment, prepared for by the two years of forgetting.

The narrator does not say this. The chapter ends on the forgetting and leaves the reader to hold it — as Joseph must have held it, day after day, in the prison, waiting for someone to remember.

---

### Joseph's plea — the first self-disclosure

**זְכַרְתַּנִי אִתְּךָ כַּאֲשֶׁר יִיטַב לְךָ וְעָשִׂיתָ-נָּא עִמָּדִי חָסֶד** — "Remember me. Show me covenant-faithfulness and mention me to Pharaoh."

Verse 14 contains Joseph's first open, desperate, explicitly personal appeal. For two chapters he has worked, refused, managed, endured. He has said nothing about what was done to him. And now, with the cupbearer restored in three days' time and a connection to Pharaoh newly visible, Joseph speaks his need.

The word **חֶסֶד** (*chesed*) — covenant-faithfulness — appears here in a context that is striking. In chapter 39:21, *chesed* was what God extended to Joseph in prison — the divine covenant-faithfulness that accompanied him in his lowest moment. Now Joseph uses the same word to ask a *human being* — an Egyptian official, a man with no covenant relationship to Joseph, a stranger — to show him *chesed*. He is asking for the kind of loyal, responding love that belongs to covenant relationship, from someone who owes him nothing.

This is either an extraordinary act of hope or an act of naivety — or both. Joseph has done something genuinely valuable for this man: he has interpreted his dream accurately (which the cupbearer will know only in retrospect, but the tone of the interpretation suggests he believed it). In the social logic of the ancient Near East, such a gift creates a debt of loyalty. Joseph is calling in that debt, framing it in the language of *chesed* — the deepest word for relational loyalty he has available.

The appeal works perfectly, and it fails completely. Joseph correctly identifies that the cupbearer has reason to help him, and correctly identifies *chesed* as the quality the situation calls for. The cupbearer walks free on the third day and forgets everything. The *chesed* Joseph asked for was not extended. And yet — as the chapter 39 usage of *chesed* has already established — God's *chesed* was not dependent on any human intermediary. The human agent fails; the divine one does not.

---

### The offense that mirrors Joseph's — *chateu*

**וַיֶּחֶטְאוּ מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם וְהָאֹפֶה לַאֲדֹנֵיהֶם** — "The cupbearer and the baker gave offense to their master."

The verb **חָטָא** (*chata'*) — to sin, to give offense, to miss the mark — opens the chapter. The cupbearer and baker *chateu* against their lord.

This is the same root Joseph used in chapter 39:9 when he told Potiphar's wife: **וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים** — "I would sin against God." He refused to *chata'*. They committed *chata'*.

The irony is structural and deliberate. The men who offended their master and ended up in prison are the men Joseph, who refused to offend, is now imprisoned alongside. The man who committed no *chata'* shares a cell with the men who did. The moral order has inverted: innocence and guilt occupy the same space. Joseph's integrity in chapter 39 led to the prison; their actual offense in chapter 40 led to the same prison.

This connection — the same word linking Joseph's refusal in 39:9 to the officials' offense in 40:1 — is the narrator's way of framing the injustice without editorializing it. Joseph is here unjustly; they are here justly. He serves them; they are served by him. The inversion is so complete it requires no commentary. The reader is invited to feel the moral weight of it.

---

### Covenant-faithfulness in a prison cell — the social architecture of chesed

In verse 14, Joseph asks the cupbearer for *chesed*. But the request itself — *if it goes well for you, please remember me* — is introduced by a beautiful conditional construction: **כִּי אִם-זְכַרְתַּנִי** — "if only you will remember me with you." The phrase **אִתְּךָ** ("with you") is literally "remember me *with yourself*" — keep me in the place where you keep things that matter.

The social architecture of *chesed* in the ancient world operated through networks of obligation and reciprocity that the Bible both assumes and partially reframes. Joseph, as a Hebrew slave in an Egyptian prison, has no legal standing, no social capital, no official patron. He is off every grid that matters. His only currency is what he has just done: he has used a God-given gift to serve two men who needed something he could provide.

He asks for nothing in return except to be remembered. *Keep me with you. Mention me to Pharaoh. Say: there is a man.*

The request is entirely legitimate. It is also entirely contingent on the cupbearer's *chesed* — a quality the narrative has already shown can be absent even in people who have every reason to demonstrate it. Joseph asks for *chesed* and receives forgetting. And God's *chesed*, which has accompanied him since chapter 39, does not require the cupbearer to remember in order to function. It simply waits.

---

### Structure: the paired dream cycle

The chapter has a precise formal structure that rewards attention:

- **Frame** (vv.1-4): offense, imprisonment, Joseph assigned to serve
- **Occasion** (v.5): one night, two dreams
- **Trigger** (vv.6-8): Joseph sees trouble, asks, they answer, he responds — "Tell me"
- **First dream and interpretation** (vv.9-15): cupbearer, three branches, three days, restoration — *and his plea*
- **Second dream and interpretation** (vv.16-19): baker, three baskets, three days, execution
- **Fulfillment** (vv.20-22): Pharaoh's birthday, both interpretations confirmed
- **Final beat** (v.23): the forgetting

The structure is a diptych: two dreams, two interpretations, two outcomes. The diptych form mirrors the content — the same phrase, two different meanings; the same number (three days), two different fates. The chapter teaches its theology through structure: the same God, the same interpreter, the same timeline — and two entirely different outcomes, entirely at Pharaoh's discretion, entirely as the dreams declared. Joseph's accuracy is 100%. Joseph's freedom remains 0%.

The gap between those two facts — perfect prophetic accuracy, continued imprisonment — is where the theology lives. The gifts of God are not immediately redemptive in the human sense. They are real, they are true, they are useful to others. They are not yet useful to Joseph himself. Not yet. Not for two more years.

---

### Manuscript observations

Leningrad Codex folio 23r (GEN_040_page_001.jpg) carries the chapter transition from Genesis 39 to Genesis 40 clearly visible in the right column. The parasha section marker — the characteristic *peh* or *samekh* of the Masoretic paragraph system — is visible between the final verse of chapter 39 and the opening of chapter 40, with the first word of chapter 40:1 — **וַיְהִי** — opening the new section. Folio 23 is marked at the top of the page, legible in the manuscript image.

The opening phrase of 40:1 — **וַיֶּחֶטְאוּ מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם** — is clearly pointed in the Tiberian system, the *chet* of *chateu* marked with its characteristic dot, the *waw*-consecutive construction of the narrative sequence visible. The repeated verb **חָטָא** in its various conjugated forms throughout the Joseph cycle is consistently pointed throughout the Leningrad Codex text.

The key interpretive phrase in verse 8 — **הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים** — is legible in the right column of folio 23r, the interrogative *heh* prefix on *halo'* clearly pointed, the plural **פִּתְרֹנִים** (*pitronim*) with its Tiberian pointing visible. This is the central theological claim of the chapter and its placement in the manuscript, near the top of the right column, gives it visual prominence.

The phrase **גֻּנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי** in verse 15 — the intensifying infinitive absolute — is visible with full pointing in the center column, the doubled *gimmel-nun-bet* root clearly readable. The word **בּוֹר** (*bor*, pit/dungeon) immediately following is pointed with the distinctive *o*-vowel of the noun form.

The repeated phrase **יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ** across verses 13 and 19 is visible in parallel positions in the manuscript column. The four-word variation in verse 19 — **מֵעָלֶיךָ** — is legible, distinguishing the two interpretations at the manuscript level as clearly as at the semantic one.

Folio 23v (GEN_040_page_002.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 40 and the opening of Genesis 41. The chapter's final verse — **וְלֹא-זָכַר שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת-יוֹסֵף וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ** — is legible in the left column of folio 23v, the two verbs of forgetting — *lo zakhar* and *vayishkachehu* — appearing in close proximity with full Tiberian vocalization. The chapter transition into Genesis 41 is marked with the parasha section indicator.

The Codex Vaticanus page 38 (pa_0038; GEN_040_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the Gen 40 material in its center column region, with the chapter marker **CAP XLV** visible in the right margin — reflecting the Vaticanus chapter numbering system, which differs from the modern Masoretic chapter divisions. The uncial Greek text runs continuously without word spacing, the characteristic ligatures of the 4th-century hand visible throughout.

Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants for Genesis 40:

**v.1** — Vaticanus reads **ἥμαρτον** ("they sinned/gave offense") for the MT's *chateu*, using the standard Greek moral vocabulary without the specific resonance of *chata'* as "missing the mark." The LXX here reads **ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος** ("the chief wine-pourer") and **ὁ ἀρχισιτοποιός** ("the chief bread-maker") — both compound Greek nouns that are Vaticanus's preferred rendering of the Hebrew *sar* + office titles throughout this chapter.

**v.8** — The theological center piece: Vaticanus renders **οὐχὶ διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἡ διασάφησις αὐτῶν ἐστιν** — "Is not through God the explanation (*diasaphēsis*) of them?" The word **διασάφησις** (from *diasapheō*, to make clear, to explain thoroughly, to clarify completely) is a stronger word than mere interpretation — it implies full disclosure, thorough clarification. The LXX translator has chosen a word that emphasizes divine *transparency*, the making-plain of what was dark, rather than simply translation between codes.

**v.13** — Vaticanus renders **ἀποκαταστήσει σε** ("will restore you") for the MT's image of Pharaoh "lifting your head" — the LXX smooths the idiom into plain speech. The restoration vocabulary in Greek (*apokathistēmi* — to restore to a former state) is theologically rich in the LXX and NT, where the same verb is used for eschatological restoration. Using it here lends a subtle eschatological coloring to the cupbearer's restoration in Vaticanus that is absent from the MT.

**v.15** — Vaticanus renders the key phrase **κλοπῇ ἐκλάπην** ("I was stolen by theft") — a cognate construction mirroring the Hebrew infinitive absolute *gunnov gunnabti*, preserving the intensifying force of the doubled root. The Greek translator has found an exact structural equivalent: noun of the same root + finite verb. This is careful translation work, and the Vaticanus scribe has preserved it without simplification.

**v.19** — The deadly addition **ἀπὸ σοῦ** ("from off you") matches the Hebrew **מֵעָלֶיךָ** exactly. Vaticanus preserves the terrible double meaning at this point, rendering it **αἰρεῖ Φαραὼ τὴν κεφαλήν σου ἀπὸ σοῦ** — "Pharaoh will take your head away from you." The Greek *airō* ("to lift, to take up, to carry away, to remove") is the same verb used for the cupbearer's interpretation in v.13 — **ἀρεῖ Φαραὼ τὴν κεφαλήν σου** — making the verbal echo exact in Greek as well as in Hebrew.

**v.23** — Vaticanus reads **οὐκ ἐμνήσθη δὲ ὁ ἀρχιοινοχόος τοῦ Ιωσηφ ἀλλ᾽ ἐπελάθετο αὐτοῦ** — "But the chief wine-pourer did not remember Joseph — rather, he forgot him." The adversative **ἀλλά** ("but/rather") between the two verbs strengthens the contrast and gives the forgetting an almost willful quality: not only did he fail to remember — he *instead* forgot. The LXX formulation is, if anything, slightly harsher than the MT in its depiction of the cupbearer's failure.

Vaticanus page 39 (pa_0039; GEN_040_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the transition from Genesis 40 to Genesis 41, with the chapter marker **CAP XLVII** visible in the right margin. The opening of the Genesis 41 text — Pharaoh's dreams — begins in the right column of page 39 in the Vaticanus uncial hand.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 23r–23v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 23r (GEN_040_page_001.jpg, PDF page 44) carries the end of Genesis 39 and the opening of Genesis 40, with the parasha section marker visible at the chapter transition. The text of Genesis 40:1 through approximately verse 15-16 occupies the central columns of folio 23r. Folio 23v (GEN_040_page_002.jpg, PDF page 45) carries Genesis 40:16 through verse 23 and the opening of Genesis 41, with the parasha section marker visible at the chapter transition into Genesis 41.
